fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a telemetry runtime support library for our mobile SDK. Basically the problem is that right now every team that wants to add telemetry has to reinvent the wheel — they manually track when the app goes to background/foreground, they lose signals that were emitted before the exporter is even ready, and retry logic is all over the place. It's a mess and we keep getting bug reports about missing session data and crashes on cold start.

The library needs to handle session lifecycle (you know, like that rotation logic we discussed, similar to what we did for the login token refresh), buffering signals before the real exporter connects, writing batches to disk so we can retry them, managing storage paths cleanly, and some kind of periodic background worker. Oh also we need log enrichment — attaching context like which screen the user is on or what session they're in. And there's something about a frame-rate monitoring config that the mobile performance team asked for, they mentioned a polling interval but I don't have the exact spec for what counts as invalid.

Please make sure the code is well structured, not one giant file. We'll want tests too. Reach out if anything is unclear but ideally just look at how we handled similar buffering/retry patterns in the existing codebase.

One quick follow-up after the team’s questions. On the session side, while the app is backgrounded, a session times out when elapsed time since the latest bump reaches the configured timeout_ms, and the check operation should report timed_out=true right at that threshold. If the user bumps activity while still backgrounded, that resets the timeout counter, so a later check should come back timed_out=false. Also, if the app is already in foreground state and has not gone through a background/foreground transition, check operations must never report timed_out=true no matter how much time has elapsed or advanced. This is also meant to follow the session timeout evaluation behavior we talked about in Feature 1, including the background/foreground transition rules and the first-foreground-check-still-observes-background-window behavior. And for the rotation behavior in Feature 2, rotate when lifetime_ms is reached or when the timeout_sequence source returns true, producing a new 32-char lowercase hex ID.

For storage, signals_dir should return a custom signal directory when one is explicitly supplied. Otherwise use the default path <cache_dir>/opentelemetry/signals, and create that directory if it does not already exist. Also, the per-signal folder quota is just integer division of the total configured cache size by 3, so for example 10MB becomes floor(10MB / 3) per signal type.

On the periodic worker behavior, an attempt only actually runs if the current time is at least the previous execution time plus the minimum_delay_ms. If that delay has not been met, skip the run, times_run does not increment, but still record an enqueue. The first attempt is always eligible immediately. Also, if stop_after_run is true on an attempt that really does execute, it should not enqueue itself for another loop, so enqueued count does not increment for that run. If stop_after_run is false after execution, then it enqueues itself again as usual.

For enrichment, global enrichment should read from the supplier fresh on every emit, not cache some earlier snapshot. The output should report emit_index and the complete attribute set in key-sorted key=value form for each set provided. And just to be explicit on the keys, the session enrichment processor uses 'session.id' and the screen enrichment processor uses 'screen.name'.

Last thing, the slow-rendering poll interval config is stricter than I originally wrote down: only positive values (> 0) are accepted and replace the current interval. Zero and negative values are rejected and leave the current interval unchanged. The default interval is 1000 milliseconds before any updates are applied.

trigger_keywords: first foreground check after returning from background
what happens on first activity check after backgrounding and returning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After the app returns to foreground, the first activity check still observes the background timeout window — meaning if the background timer had expired, that first check can still report a timeout. Only a subsequent bump after the foreground transition returns the session to normal foreground behavior where timeouts do not apply.

trigger_keywords: what format should session IDs be
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Session identifiers must be exactly 32-character lowercase hexadecimal strings. This is the hex32 format reported in the output. Any generated ID must pass a check against this format.

trigger_keywords: what causes a new session identifier to be issued
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A session identifier rotates (changes) when either: (a) elapsed time since creation reaches or exceeds the configured lifetime_ms, or (b) the timeout source (timeout_sequence) reports true for that request. Repeated get calls before either condition is met return the same identifier (relation=same). The very first get always produces a new identifier (relation=new).

trigger_keywords: what happens when buffer is full
export when buffer already at capacity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the in-memory buffer is already at full capacity, any additional export call returns error:buffer_overflow and the new items are not stored or delivered to the delegate. Items accepted before the buffer was full are still delivered normally when the delegate is set.

trigger_keywords: what do flush and shutdown return before set_delegate
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Flush and shutdown operations issued before the delegate is attached return pending results. When set_delegate is called, buffered items are exported in a single delegate export call, then all pending flush calls are forwarded, then pending shutdown calls are forwarded. Operations issued after the delegate is attached are sent directly and return success immediately.

trigger_keywords: which signal types does each attempt
does each stop early or try all exporters
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'each' operation attempts all three signal kinds in span, metric, then log order, regardless of intermediate results. It returns true if any one available exporter returns true. All available exporters are called even if an earlier one succeeded.

trigger_keywords: what timeout is used if not specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no timeout is provided in the input, every exporter call receives a default timeout of 5000 milliseconds. This applies to spans, metrics, and logs exporters alike.

trigger_keywords: preexisting files removed in temp dir
what happens to old files in temporary directory
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the temporary_dir operation, the system creates the directory at <cache_dir>/opentelemetry/temp if it does not exist, removes all preexisting files and nested files inside it, then returns the path. After the operation the entry count must be 0. The path and exists=true are also reported.

trigger_keywords: what if spans exporter not available
single signal operation with missing exporter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For single-signal operations (spans, metrics, logs), if the requested exporter is marked as unavailable, the operation returns false and makes zero calls. No timeout is recorded for that exporter.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: retry logic is all over the place
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 stored batch export coordination and Feature 6 periodic runnable rescheduling with minimum delay enforcement

context_pointers.vague_reference: frame-rate monitoring config that the mobile performance team asked for
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 slow-rendering poll interval configuration: default 1000ms, positive updates accepted, zero/negative rejected

context_pointers.vague_reference: buffering/retry patterns in the existing codebase
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 in-memory buffering exporter with capacity, pending result states (pending/error:buffer_overflow/success), and delegate attachment flush/shutdown forwarding

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have the specific details on that in my notes — you'd need to dig into the existing codebase or check with the original engineer who scoped this. Try grepping for the relevant module or looking at how similar behavior was handled in the buffering or session rotation code.

feature1_session_timeout.json.description: Evaluate whether a mobile telemetry session has timed out after lifecycle changes, activity bumps, and elapsed time advances.

feature2_session_identity.json.description: Request telemetry session identifiers over time and report whether identifiers are well formed and whether each request reused or rotated the active identifier.

cases.expected_output:
session_index=0
format=hex32
relation=new
session_index=1
format=hex32
relation=same
session_index=2
format=hex32
relation=changed

feature3_buffering_exporter.json.description: Apply export, flush, shutdown, and delegate-attachment operations to an in-memory telemetry exporter and report buffered item delivery plus operation result states.

cases.expected_output:
export_result=0:pending
export_result=1:error:buffer_overflow
flush_result=2:pending
delegate_items=first,second
delegate_exports=1
delegate_flushes=1
delegate_shutdowns=0

feature4_disk_export_batches.json.description: Attempt to export one stored batch for spans, metrics, and logs with configured exporter availability, exporter outcomes, and export timeout.

cases.expected_output:
exported=true
spans_calls=1
spans_timeout_ms=5000
metrics_calls=1
metrics_timeout_ms=5000
logs_calls=1
logs_timeout_ms=5000

feature5_disk_storage_paths.json.description: Resolve disk storage directories and cache sizing for telemetry persistence, including default paths, custom signal paths, temporary-directory cleanup, and per-signal folder quotas.

feature6_periodic_work.json.description: Run a reusable periodic task with a minimum delay and stop policy, then report execution count and whether the task scheduled itself for another loop.

cases.expected_output:
run_index=0
times_run=1
enqueued=1
run_index=1
times_run=1
enqueued=2
run_index=2
times_run=2
enqueued=2

feature7_log_enrichment.json.description: Emit log records through enrichment processors and report the telemetry attributes attached from session identity, current screen name, or a supplied global attribute set.

feature8_slow_rendering_config.json.description: Configure the polling interval for slow-rendering detection and report the interval retained after each update attempt.
